reasoning: D and B are the two strongest, both pairing the bottom-up demand chain with a quantified independent BLS top-down cross-check and explicitly handling the city-vs-metro fork. D wins on depth and bias awareness: it is the only answer that names multiple specific traps (anchoring on the textbook figure, survivorship, confirmation/salience from famous engineer-CEOs), runs two numerically-grounded cross-checks (BLS plus Piano Technicians Guild), and frames (b) as an explicit Bayesian partition with a defensible ~28% (range 22-35%). B is a very close second, with the most honest reconciliation of its two methods (noting they disagree 2-3x and shading toward the lower blend to land near the classic ~50 city figure) and crisp calibration, though it names fewer biases. A is solid and well-calibrated but only gestures at the top-down BLS count without computing it, weakening its cross-check. C is weakest: it overstates engineering alone as ~32% to inflate STEM to 38-40% (above the survey literature), does no numeric external cross-check, and ignores the metro definition in its verdict.
